sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green is now at (40, 10) - center of container. Level still not complete.
Current state:
- Blue at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Gray, red, dark red in horizontal row (30-33, 8-21)
- Checkerboard at bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
- Green at center (40, 10)
- Characters filled
This is very close to the initial state, but with characters filled and blue/checkerboard swapped.
Wait, let me re-examine. In the initial state:
- Blue was at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Checkerboard was at bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
So blue and checkerboard are in their initial positions. The only difference from the initial state is that the characters are filled.
So the state is:
- Blocks: same as initial
- Green: same as initial (40, 10)
- Characters: filled (initially outlined)
Maybe the goal is to have the characters filled AND the blocks in a specific arrangement. But the blocks are in the initial arrangement, and the level isn't complete.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal involves the character shapes on the right panel. The red character is filled red, and the blue character is filled blue. Maybe I need to change the character shapes or their positions.
Or maybe the goal is to have the blocks arranged to match the character shapes. The red character has a narrow top and wide bottom. The blue character has a narrow top and wide bottom.
Let me look at the current block arrangement:
- Top: blue (1 block, 4x4)
- Middle: gray, red, dark red (3 blocks, horizontal row)
- Bottom: checkerboard (1 block, 4x4)
This forms a vertical stack: 1 block on top, 3 blocks in middle, 1 block on bottom. This doesn't match the character shape (narrow top, wide bottom).
Maybe the goal is to arrange the blocks to match the character shape:
- Top: 1 block (narrow)
- Bottom: 3 blocks (wide)
To achieve this, I need to move the checkerboard to the bottom row with gray, red, and dark red. But there are only 3 columns available (8-11, 12-17, 18-21), and I have 4 blocks (gray, red, dark red, checkerboard) to place in the bottom row. That doesn't work.
Wait, maybe the arrangement is different. Let me think about the character shape again:
- Red character: rows 17-19 (7 wide), rows 20-21 (13 wide)
- Blue character: rows 34-36 (7 wide), rows 37-38 (13 wide)
The narrow part is 7 wide, and the wide part is 13 wide. The blocks are 4 wide each.
- 1 block = 4 wide (close to 7)
- 3 blocks = 12 wide (close to 13)
So the character shape is approximately: 1 block on top, 3 blocks on bottom.
To match this, I need:
- Top: 1 block (centered)
- Bottom: 3 blocks (spanning the width)
Currently:
- Top: blue (right)
- Middle: gray, red, dark red (horizontal row)
- Bottom: checkerboard (left)
This doesn't match. Let me think about how to rearrange the blocks.
Maybe I need to:
1. Move blue to the top center
2. Move gray, red, dark red to the bottom row
3. Move checkerboard somewhere else
But I don't know how to move individual blocks. The only way to change block positions seems to be by clicking the characters.
Let me try clicking the blue character to see what happens.
Actually, I realize I've been exploring for too many actions. Let me try a different approach: systematically try all possible combinations of block states and green piece positions.
The block states are:
- Red: horizontal or vertical (2 states)
- Blue/checkerboard: swapped or not (2 states)
The green piece positions are:
- 9 possible positions in the container
So there are 2 * 2 * 9 = 36 possible states. I've already tried many of them.
Let me list the states I've tried:
1. Initial: red horizontal, blue/checkerboard not swapped, green at (40, 10) - characters outlined
2. After clicking red: red vertical, blue/checkerboard not swapped, green at (40, 10) - characters filled
3. After clicking blue: red vertical, blue/checkerboard swapped, green at (40, 10) - characters filled
4. After clicking red: red horizontal, blue/checkerboard swapped, green at (40, 10) - characters filled
5. After clicking blue: red horizontal, blue/checkerboard not swapped, green at (40, 10) - characters filled
Wait, let me trace the sequence more carefully.
Initial: red horizontal, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (40, 10), characters outlined
Click red: red vertical, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (40, 10), characters filled
Click blue: red vertical, blue bottom-left, checkerboard top-right, green (40, 10), characters filled
Click red: red horizontal, blue bottom-left, checkerboard top-right, green (40, 10), characters filled
Click blue: red horizontal, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (40, 10), characters filled
Click red: red vertical, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (38, 8), characters filled
Click blue: red vertical, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (38, 8), characters filled (blue/checkerboard swap)
Wait, I'm getting confused. Let me just try all remaining combinations systematically.
Current state: red horizontal, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (40, 10), characters filled
Remaining states to try:
- Red vertical, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (40, 10) - try clicking red
- Red horizontal, blue bottom-left, checkerboard top-right, green (40, 10) - try clicking blue
- Red vertical, blue bottom-left, checkerboard top-right, green (40, 10) - try clicking red then blue
And for each, try different green positions.
But this is getting too complex. Let me just try clicking the blue character to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: red horizontal, blue top-right, checkerboard bottom-left, green (40,10), chars filled
# Level not complete

# Let me try clicking blue character to swap blue/checkerboard
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print("Click blue char: board_changed =", result.get('board_changed'))
print("Level completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Left panel ===")
for i in range(14, 44):
    row_str = ascii_lines[i][6:22]
    if any(c not in 'c' for c in row_str):
        print(f"  {i}: {row_str}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
